coredata.dc:title: Fluid-like carbon dots-based ionic fluid towards high efficient lubricant nanoadditive of polyethylene glycol
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Tao
Liu, Dan
Liu, Shenggao
coredata.dc:description: Solid polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots as a precursor were synthesized using citric acid and poly(sodium 4-styrene sulfonate) via pyrolysis route, which were then capped by block copolymer to produce solvent-free carbon dots-based ionic fluid (CDs-PSS-M2070). The tribological performance of CDs-PSS-M2070 under different concentrations and loads was evaluated in polyethylene glycol (PEG). The results verified that CDs-PSS-M2070 efficiently improved the friction-reducing and wear-resistance performance as well as the load-bearing capacity of PEG in a broad load. The addition of 1.0% CDs-PSS-M2070 reduced wear volume of 82.8% and friction coefficient of 73.5% under the load of 392 N. Owing to the excellent dispersion of carbon dots ionic fluid in PEG, CDs-PSS-M2070 could continue to transfer to the frictional interface and form a continuous carbon-rich oil-film, thereby enhancing friction-reducing and wear-resistance abilities efficiently.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanofluid
Morphology
Tribological properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots
Synthesis of the carbon dots-based nanofluid

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM micrographs and corresponding size distribution of CDs-PSS-Na (a–c), and CDs-PSS-M2070 (d–f).
FT-IR spectra of CDs-PSS-Na, M2070 and CDs-PSS-M2070.
XRD patterns of CDs-PSS-Na, M2070 and CDs-PSS-M2070.
XPS survey and N 1s spectra of carbon dots (a, b) and C 1s fine spectra of CDs-PSS-Na (c), CDs-PSS-M2070 (d).
The DSC trace of M2070 (a), CDs-PSS-M2070 (b).
The TGA traces of CDs-PSS and CDs-PSS-M2070.
(a) Friction coefficient curves and (b) wear volumes of PEG and PEG with CDs-PSS-M2070.
(a) Mean friction coefficient curves and (b) wear volumes of PEG and PEG with CDs-PSS-M2070 at different loads.
SEM micrographs, 3D profiles and the corresponding 2D depth profiles of wear scars (a, c, e) PEG and 1.0% CDs-PSS-M2070 in PEG (b, d, f) at 392 N.
Raman spectral mapping and corresponding spectra of worn surface lubricated by 1.0% CDs-PSS-M2070 in PEG.
Curve-fitted XPS spectra of the worn surface lubricated by neat PEG (a), PEG with 1.0% CDs-PSS-M2070 (b).
Schematic illustration of the lubricating mechanism of CDs-PSS-M2070.
Schematic presentation of the synthesis of the carbon dots-based nanofluid.
The elemental composition of CDs-PSS-Na and CDs-PSS-M2070 in XPS full spectra.
The percentage contents of C1s three fitted peaks and corresponding C/O ratio of CDs-PSS-Na and CDs-PSS-M2070.

head.ce:title: Fluid-like carbon dots-based ionic fluid towards high efficient lubricant nanoadditive of polyethylene glycol

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Related Technologies
Zhejiang Key Laboratory of Marine Materials and Protective Technologies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solid polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots as a precursor were synthesized using citric acid and poly(sodium 4-styrene sulfonate) via pyrolysis route, which were then capped by block copolymer to produce solvent-free carbon dots-based ionic fluid (CDs-PSS-M2070). The tribological performance of CDs-PSS-M2070 under different concentrations and loads was evaluated in polyethylene glycol (PEG). The results verified that CDs-PSS-M2070 efficiently improved the friction-reducing and wear-resistance performance as well as the load-bearing capacity of PEG in a broad load. The addition of 1.0% CDs-PSS-M2070 reduced wear volume of 82.8% and friction coefficient of 73.5% under the load of 392 N. Owing to the excellent dispersion of carbon dots ionic fluid in PEG, CDs-PSS-M2070 could continue to transfer to the frictional interface and form a continuous carbon-rich oil-film, thereby enhancing friction-reducing and wear-resistance abilities efficiently.

ce:section.ce:para: Boundary lubrication is the critical state before the transition from liquid lubrication to dry friction, which is considered as the most severe lubrication condition owing to the direct partial surface contact between the asperities accounting for the wear of equipment and machines as well as energy loss [1,2]. Consequently, lubricating oils containing anti-wear and anti-friction additives are indispensable to improve wear-resistance and reduce friction under such harsh conditions, which can effectively protect the equipment and machines and extended their service cycles [3–5]. Commonly, traditional lubricating oils are composed of mineral oils and anti-wear additives containing sulfur, phosphorus, halogen, which both not only cause severe environmental problems but also cannot meet modern demands [6,7]. In recent years, synthetic oil, such as polyethylene glycol (PEG), has been explored as base oil due to its environmental friendliness and biodegradability, which can be used in high-temperature lubricants, grease, gear oils, brake fluids and metalworking fluids, etc. [8,9]. Unfortunately, due to its polar nature, PEG shows weak compatibility with traditional lubricant additives. Therefore, developing an eco-friendly, efficient anti-wear and friction-reducing lubricant additive is of great significance and demand for practical applications in the field of tribology. To date, nanomaterials have been widely explored as potential green lubricant additives and previous reports demonstrate that the introduced nanoparticles, including metal oxides, ceramics, metal dichalcogenides, graphene, carbon nanotube and fullerene, etc., are able to effectively improve the lubricity of base fluids [10–17]. In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted the attention of many researchers due to their unique properties and small size (<10 nm), and many studies have reported their tribological performance as lubricant additives [18–24]. Especially, owing to its facile preparing methods and versatile chemical structures, which are both outstanding strengths for CDs to meet various needs in the field of lubrication.

For the long-term applications of nanomaterials in lubricating oils, several issues must be addressed. Firstly, the biggest challenge to apply nanoparticles as additives in base fluids is how to achieve a stable nanoparticle dispersion under static condition (storing state) and dynamic condition (working state: high pressure/temperature/shearing), which is crucial to achieving their full tribological potential under boundary lubrication [25,26]. Secondly, how to achieve superior tribological performance under the broad load is of high interest, which contributes to broadening the diverse applications in the field of lubrication [26,28].

Surface modification and functionalization of nanoparticles in Nanoscale ionic materials (NIMs) is not only a promising approach to address their dispersion stability issue, but also can regulate their physicochemical properties [28,29]. Commonly, NIMs can be regarded as solvent-free supramolecular systems, where inorganic cores are densely bonded by an organic ionic liquid shell, consequently forming hybrid ionic nanofluids [30]. To date, various solvent-free nanofluids have been synthesized using Fe3O4, TiO2, Graphene oxides, SiO2, ZnO, the hybrids as inorganic cores and the hydrophilic organic compound or polymer as outer shell [28–34]. These new kinds of materials possess improved water dispersibility and tunable wettability, hydrophilicity and electrical responsiveness (which can be applied as nanofiller in composites), lubricant additives, key components of gas separation membranes and so on [28–34].

Based on synthetic chemistry and lubrication mechanism, it can be deduced that the designable outer ionic liquid shell of solvent-free nanofluids provide nanoparticles with tunable tribological performance, while specially shaped inorganic cores, such as spherical structure, can endow them with “rolling and self-healing” effects, thus contributing to enhanced tribological performance. So far, conducting carbon dots have been reported and show stable redox behavior within an appreciable voltage regime [30,34]. Unfortunately, the conducting carbon dots have never been exploited and used as anti-wear and anti-friction additives. One of the biggest challenges is the conducting carbon dots cannot be dispersed in PEG due to their terrible compatibility.

Herein, fluid-like polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots-based nanofluid was synthesized by using conducting carbon dots as a precursor. A step-wise surface modification technique was employed to convert powdered polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots into carbon dots-based nanofluid at room temperature. Thereafter, amino-terminated block copolymer was densely bonded on the polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots surface to regulate the polarity and compatibility, thus contributing to the potential applications in base fluid in terms of the polar medium of PEG. It is worth mentioning that fluid-like carbon dots ionic fluid has never been applied to PEG as lubricant nanoadditive. The microstructure and thermal properties were analyzed using Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and Differential scanning calorimeter (DSC). The dispersion stability was evaluated with a sedimentation experiment at the ambient temperature and lubrication condition. The frictional performance was evaluated by the four-ball tester under boundary lubrication and the corresponding lubrication mechanism was discussed based on the combined results of Raman spectroscopy and XPS analyses.

Citric acid monohydrate (Aladdin, 99.5%), poly(sodium 4-styrene sulfonate) (Aladdin, 99.5%). Polyether amine (Mw = 2000 g·mol−1) was provided by the Huntsman Co., Ltd. (USA). Polyethylene glycol (PEG, Mw = 200 g·mol−1) was provided by Aladdin Chemical Corporation, which was used as received. Deionized water was used in all the experiments. All the other used organic solvents in this work were provided by Shanghai Chemical Reagent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Solid polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots were facilely synthesized by one-pot pyrolysis route between citric acid and poly(sodium 4-styrene sulfonate), which were mixed with deionized water at ambient temperature to obtain a transparent solution. Thereafter, most of the liquid was removed by rotary evaporation, and then the raw mixture was pyrolyzed for 2 h at 200 °C in a flask under reduced pressure. When the product was cooled to ambient temperature, deionized water was added to dilute the solution and then the sediments in the diluted solution were removed by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min). The supernatant was dialyzed against a mixed solution of deionized water and ethanol for 48 h (MWCO = 1000) to purify the as-prepared polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots (denoted as CDs-PSS-Na).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the carbon dots-based nanofluid
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the theory and experience, there are considerable amounts of sulfonate groups on the surface of polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots, which are favorable for the acid-base reaction between sulfonate group and amine group to produce fluid-like carbon-dots-based nanofluids. For the synthesis of hydrophilic carbon dots nanofluid, 0.4 g of as-prepared polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots (CDs-PSS-Na) was diluted in deionized water to form a transparent brown solution. Thereafter, the as-obtained aqueous solution was passed through a strong acidic cationic exchange resin three times to convert the Na+ ions to H+ ions completely (denoted as CDs-PSS). Polyether amine, such as M2070, was firstly dissolved in deionized water with a concentration of 10.7 wt%, and then dropwise added in the above-mentioned aqueous solution containing polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots within 60 min. Finally, the solvent-free hydrophilic carbon dots nanofluid was synthesized based on the acid-base reaction between sulfonate group of polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots and amine group of M2070 (denoted as CDs-PSS-M2070). The structures and synthesis routes of carbon dots nanofluid are depicted in Scheme 1 .

The tribological performance of CDs-PSS-M2070 was analyzed using a four-ball tribometer (Tenkey, China) under boundary lubrication conditions. About 10 mL of as-prepared PEG base oil containing CDs-PSS-M2070 was poured into the oil cup, which was firstly cleaned alternately by petroleum ether and ethanol. The tribo-test was examined by a four-ball tribometer with a ball-on-ball configuration, in which the above steel ball was rotated against three fixed GCr steel balls below. The diameter, roughness and hardness of the steel balls are 12.7 mm, 0.032 μm and 59–61 HRC respectively. The anti-wear properties were measured by the size of the worn area/volume on the steel balls, while the friction coefficient curve was recorded automatically by a computer linked to the four-ball tribometer. Each frictional result was repeated three times under the given lubrication conditions to ensure repeatability and reliability. Thereafter, the wear volume (WV) was estimated with the below equation:
V =
π h
6
3
d 2
4
+
h 2
where
h = r −
r 2
−
d 2
4
d is the wear scar diameter and r is the radius of the ball.

The 3D morphologies, elemental compositions and corresponding distributions of carbon elements on the wear scars of the CDs-PSS-M207 were recorded by the combination of Zeiss LSM700 laser microscope and Zeiss EVO18 scanning electron microscope (SEM). Raman spectra were used to characterize the defect structure on the wear scars with a laser source ranged from 2000 to 500 cm−1. In addition, the XPS and Raman spectral mapping were used to characterize the worn surface for exploring the possible tribo-chemical reactions and lubrication mechanism.

The TEM images of the conducting CDs-PSS-Na and nanofluid of CDs-PSS-M2070 were shown in Fig. 1 . Two kinds of carbon dots have a nearly spherical morphology without aggregation. The size distribution of CDs-PSS-Na showed the smallest size, which ranged from 1.30 to 2.50 nm with an average size of 1.83 nm. By contrast, the surface modification of CDs-PSS-Na with M2070 led to the obvious increase of the average size of the CDs-PSS-M2070, which had a size range of 3.30–6.80 nm with an average size of 5.35 nm. This result indicated that the polymers capped on the surface of carbon dots had a significant effect on the particle size.

Fig. 2 showed the FT-IR of CDs-PSS-Na and CDs-PSS-M2070. The peak at around 1719 cm−1 verified the presence of CO in CDs-PSS-Na [23,35]. In addition, the peaks at 1177, 1126, 1036 and 1008 cm−1 were attributed to the sulfonic group of CDs-PSS-Na [27,32,34]. After the CDs-PSS-Na was converted into fluid-like CDs-PSS-M2070, the new typical peaks at 2870, 1456, 1112 cm−1 were detected, which belonged to –CH3, CN, –CH2–O–CH2– groups of M2070, suggesting the M2070 had been successfully capped on the surface of the CDs-PSS-M2070 [7,10,27,29,36,37]. Fig. 3 showed the characteristic XRD peaks of CDs-PSS-Na and CDs-PSS-M2070. The solid CDs-PSS-Na showed a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 18.14°, suggesting the CDs-PSS-Na had relatively low crystallinity. The M2070 displayed a broad and strong diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.10° with an amorphous structure. By contrast, the weak diffraction peak of CDs-PSS-Na disappeared and a broad diffraction peak at around 20° appeared for fluid-like CDs-PSS-M2070, indicating a strong chemical interaction between M2070 and CDs-PSS-Na (Fig. 3a). The results demonstrated that M2070 had strong chemical interactions with CDs-PSS-Na.

The chemical compositions of CDs-PSS-Na and CDs-PSS-M2070 were identified by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The full spectra (Fig. 4a) showed that the CDs-PSS-Na was composed of C, O, S, Na elements. After the M2070 was bonded on the surface of CDs-PSS-Na, it was found that CDs-PSS-M2070 was composed of C, O, S, N elements without Na element. The signals of N1s located at 401.8 eV were attributed to the cationic structure of N of the CDs-PSS-M2070 (Fig. 4b) [23]. Combined with the N 1s fine spectra, we could speculate the successful surface modification of the CDs-PSS-Na between sulfonate group and amine group. Tables 1 and 2
showed the elemental content of the CDs-PSS-Na and CDs-PSS-M2070, the C/O ratio (weight percentage) of CDs-PSS-M2070 was 1.95, which was slightly lower than that of CDs-PSS-Na. The differentiated ratio was due to the molecular structure of M2070 consisting of high content of alkoxy groups and its C/O ratio was close to the CDs-PSS-Na. The C 1s fine spectra of three carbon dots further demonstrated the above conclusion (Fig. 4c–d). The C 1s could be divided into three peaks at 284.8, 285.7 and 289.1 eV for CDs-PSS-Na, which were assigned to C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N and CO (Fig. 4c) [38]. By contrast, compared with the CDs-PSS-Na, the binding energy of CDs-PSS-M2070 changed slightly (Fig. 4d). According to Table 2, it was noted that, compared with CDs-PSS-Na, the percentage contents of C-C/C=C and CO in the sample decreased from 40.97% and 14.18% (CDs-PSS-Na) to 29.21% and 1.80% (CDs-PSS-M2070), while the content of C-O/C-N increased significantly from 44.85% to 68.29%. The XPS and TEM results demonstrated that M2070 had been introduced on the surface of CDs-PSS-Na, which had obvious effects on the composition and microstructure of carbon dots-based nanofluid.

The thermal properties of CDs-PSS-M2070 were investigated by DSC testing. Fig. 5 showed the melting temperature (T
m ) of CDs-PSS-M2070 was at 4.7 °C and glass transition temperature (T
g ) was −61.3 °C, which was below room temperature, indicating the as-obtained CDs-PSS-M2070 remained fluid-like feature. Furthermore, the relative solid content of carbon dots-based nanofluid was estimated by TGA testing under Ar atmosphere. Fig. 6 showed the thermal decomposition of CDs-PSS (Na+ ions of CDs-PSS-Na have been converted to H+ ions totally), CDs-PSS-M2070. CDs-PSS exhibited an apparent weight loss before 200 °C, suggesting the oxygen-containing components started to decompose at the initial stage. Weight loss caused by further heating resulted from the pyrolysis of the carbon skeleton of CDs-PSS, which had a residual mass of 32.79% at 700 °C. The CDs-PSS-M2070 showed different thermal degradation behaviors with the CDs-PSS. The weight losses between 200 and 450 °C of CDs-PSS-M2070 was attributed to the polymer decomposition of M2070. The M2070 could be totally decomposed at 700 °C (Fig. S1), while the residual mass of CDs-PSS-M2070 was 5.32%, thereby calculating the relative solid contents of CDs-PSS-M2070 was 16.22%. The fluorescence emission spectra of the as prepared CDs-PSS-M2070 was shown in Fig. S2.

Boundary lubrication is considered as the most severe lubrication condition owing to the direct contact between the asperities [5,39]. To prove the lubrication potential of CDs-PSS-M2070 nanoadditive, PEG oils with different concentrations of CDs-PSS-M2070 were tested under fixed conditions (rotate speed: 1200 rpm; temperature 75 °C; duration: 60 min; load: 392 N). The tribological properties of CDs-PSS-M2070 with concentrations of 0.25%, 0.5%, 0.75%, 1% and 1.5% in PEG were shown in Fig. 7 . The friction coefficient (CoF) curve of PEG is unstable during rubbing, which showed a relatively broad spike (>0.11) between 700 and 2600 s, thereafter, the CoF decreased gradually to a stable value of 0.11. The average CoF of curve for PEG was 0.117. The addition of M2070 showed a slightly improved CoF (0.116), but the CoF of curve was unstable as well. By contrast, the CDs-PSS-M2070 was able to reduce the friction coefficient of PEG obviously, which decreased to 0.109 (0.25%), 0.044 (0.5%), 0.043 (0.75%), 0.031 (1.0%) and 0.032 (1.5%). It was obvious that the addition of 1.0% CDs-PSS-M2070 to PEG exhibited the lowest and most stable friction coefficient curve. On the other side, the anti-wear results (Fig. 7b) showed that the wear volume (WV) of PEG could be obviously reduced when a low concentration of 0.50% CDs-PSS-M2070 was added, which reduced the wear volume of 79.8%. The WV of PEG was further reduced with the increased content of CDs-PSS-M2070. When the content reached 1.0%, the WV was reduced by 82.8%. By contrast, the addition of M2070 only reduced the wear volume by 54.3%.

To further study the tribological potential of CDs-PSS-M2070, the tribological properties of PEG with 1.0% of CDs-PSS-M2070 at different loads ranged from 96 N to 588 N were tested for three times and the corresponding results were shown in Fig. 8 (rotate speed: 1200 rpm; temperature 75 °C; duration: 60 min). Interestingly, the CDs-PSS-M2070 could efficiently improve the friction-reducing performance of PEG at a broad load. The mean CoF of PEG were 0.114 (98 N), 0.108 (196 N), 0.106 (294 N), 0.117 (392 N), 0.112 (490 N) and 0.120 (588 N). Once the CDs-PSS-M2070 was introduced, the mean CoF decreased to 0.038 (98 N), 0.035 (196 N), 0.032 (294 N), 0.040 (392 N), 0.054 (490 N) and 0.045 (588 N), indicating 66.7%, 67.6%, 69.8%, 65.8%, 51.9% and 52.5%. By contrast, when the applied load below 294 N, CDs-PSS-M2070 could not improve the anti-wear performance of PEG but even made the anti-wear performance worse. When the load was higher than 294 N, the anti-wear performance was improved obviously, which reduced WV in the ranges of 79.6–83.7%. These results suggest that applied load had an apparent effect on the load capacity performance of CDs-PSS-M2070, which also confirms the outstanding lubrication potential of CDs-PSS-M2070 with a special core-shell structure as a lubricating additive in PEG under boundary lubrication conditions.

To compare the worn areas more intuitively, SEM graphs, 3D images and the corresponding 2D depth profiles of pure PEG and PEG with 1.0% CDs-PSS-M2070 are shown in Fig. 9 . The mean wear scar diameter (WSD) on the worn steel ball lubricated by neat PEG was 1.267 mm, while wear scar diameters of PEG with 1.0% CDs-PSS-M2070 was 0.674 mm (reduced by 46.8%). The worn surface lubricated by PEG blended with 1.0% CDs-PSS-M2070 was obviously narrower and shallower than that lubricated by neat PEG, which further demonstrated the CDs-PSS-M2070 as additives having excellent anti-wear property.

It was worth mentioning that the as-prepared carbon dots-based nanofluid of CDs-PSS-M2070 was able to be dispersed well in polar PEG for over 10 months without sediments. Impressively, carbon dots-based nanofluid could keep transparent even after a harsh tribo-test under boundary lubrication conditions. The results demonstrated the excellent static and dynamic dispersion stability of solvent-free carbon dots nanofluids in PEG base oil under a storing state and work state, which played a key role in achieving the superior tribological potential of carbon dots nanoadditives under severe conditions (Fig. S3). To evaluate the dispersion stability of carbon-based nanofluids at high temperature, the CDs-PSS-M2070 was directly dispersed in PEG by magnetic stirring. The sedimentation experiment was employed to analyze the dispersion stability of the CDs-PSS-M2070 at 100 °C (Fig. S4). It was clearly that the CDs-PSS-M2070 lubricants with concentrations of 0.25%, 0.5%, 0.75%, 1% and 1.5% were able to stand for a long period of 20 days without sediment and stratification, proving the superior dispersion stability. It was noted that carbon-based nanofluids still possessed photoluminescence properties after 20 days, indicating ultrahigh dispersion stability.

Micro-Raman spectroscopy and XPS analysis were employed to characterize the worn surface, which helped understand the lubrication mechanism of CDs-PSS-M2070. It was obvious that the worn surface lubricated by PEG detected a weak and a sharp band below ~800 cm−1, which were assigned to the iron oxides, indicating a serious oxidation occurred during friction (Fig. S5) [10,39,40]. By contrast (Fig. 10 ), the spectrum of the worn surface lubricated by PEG with 1.0% CDs-PSS-M2070 showed carbon characteristic bands at ~1337 cm−1 assigned to the D band and ~1593 cm−1 assigned to the G band [41]. The result suggested the CDs-PSS-M2070 could transfer from PEG base oil to the rubbing interface during rubbing thus enhancing the tribological performance effectively.

Moreover, the nanostructure of carbonaceous tribo-film of the CDs-PSS-M2070 was further identified by Raman spectral mapping, among which the red area represented the largest area of D and G peaks (Fig. 10). The colorful areas showed irregular color distribution, suggesting that the carbonaceous oil-film was not evenly generated on the friction interface. Raman analyses strongly suggested that the carbon dots nanoadditive (CDs-PSS-M2070) deposited on the contact interface to generate a kind of carbon-rich oil-film and prevented the direct contact during rubbing, which was helpful to improve the anti-wear and friction-reducing effects of base oil. Additionally, the carbon-based tribofilm had different defect levels, which indicated the friction-induced tribo-chemical reaction between the carbon dots nanoadditive and contact surface occurred simultaneously, and the carbon deposited on the interface unevenly. This may be due to the uneven distribution of load and roughness of the contact surface during the rubbing process.

The chemical composition of oil-film covered on the worn surfaces was further characterized with XPS analysis and shown in Fig. 11 , the signal of Fe 2p detected on the worn surface lubricated by neat PEG is different from that of the worn surface lubricated by CDs-PSS-M2070 in PEG. For worn surface lubricated by PEG (Fig. 11a), the Fe 2p, C 1s and O 1s peaks were detected after tribo-test, suggested that the molecules of PEG base oil absorbed on the interface and reacted with the iron-based worn surface. C 1s fine spectrum showed a triplet-peak structure, which could be divided into three peaks at 284.6, 286.3, 287.9 eV, respectively, belonging to the C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N and CO. The O1s peaks at 530.3, 532.6 eV and Fe2p peaks at 709.8, 711.6 eV together confirmed the presence of FeO and Fe2O3 [42–44]. For the worn surface lubricated by CDs-PSS-M2070 in PEG (Fig. 11b), the signals of N 1s and S 2p could be detected on the worn surface, suggesting the CDs-PSS-M2070 deposited and reacted with the iron-based surface to produce an adsorption and tribo-reaction oil-film during the friction process. The Fe2p peak at 712.8 eV and S2p peak at 161.2 eV confirmed the presence of FeS on the worn surface in addition to FeO and Fe2O3[45,46]. Besides, the Fe sulfates could be observed on the oil-film based on the S2p peak at 168.6 eV [6]. Once the CDs-PSS-M2070 was introduced in PEG, the relative content of C-C/C=C increased in the carbon-based oil-film, which indicated the CDs-PSS-M2070 transferred to the frictional interface and played an important role in enhancing the tribological properties. Through the comparison of the analysis results above, we can speculate that the well-dispersed fluid-like carbon dots (CDs-PSS-M2070) are prone to absorb on the friction surface during the rubbing and thereby generate a carbon-rich oil-film, which is beneficial to improve the friction and wear properties of base oils (PEG) under boundary lubrication.

Based on the former analyses, the lubrication mechanism of the polyelectrolyte grafted carbon dots-based ionic fluid could be proposed. As shown in Fig. 12 , at the initial stage of the frictional test, the tribo-pairs tended to escaped electrons and form a positively charged surface, the negative moieties of carbon dots ionic fluids helped the CDs-PSS-M2070 transfer from oils to the positively charged surface and gradually generated a carbon-rich adsorption film, thereby inhibiting the direct contact of metallic frictional pairs effectively. Besides, the spherical inner core of carbon dots endowed the “rolling” effect as well, which helped reduce the coefficient of friction obviously. With the prolonging of rubbing time, the absorbed CDs-PSS-M2070 started to degrade and react with the iron-based surface to produce a tribo-film mainly consisting of iron oxides, sulfates and a layer of carbon, which not only worked as a “self-mending” effect to repair the rubbing surface but also enhanced the anti-wear performance. In addition, owing to the excellent dispersion of carbon dots ionic fluids in PEG, CDs-PSS-M2070 could continue to transfer to the frictional interface, contributing to form a continuous carbon-rich oil-film. Simultaneously, amino-terminated block copolymer capped on the carbon dots was prone to react with an iron-based interface under high temperature, high pressure, and high shear conditions, thereby forming a lubricating film with nitrogen compounds. As a result, carbon dots ionic fluids as nanoadditives in PEG can enhance friction-reducing and wear-resistance abilities efficiently.

Fluid-like carbon dots-based nanofluid, namely CDs-PSS-M2070, was synthesized based on the pyrolysis route and acid-base reaction with a solid content of 16.22%, which was demonstrated to own simultaneous excellent dispersibility and tribological performance in the polar base oil of PEG. The CDs-PSS-M2070 was systematically analyzed by TEM, FT-IR, XRD, XPS, TGA, and DSC. The tribological performance was evaluated by a four-ball tester under boundary lubrication for steel-to-steel contact. The wear volume and friction coefficient of PEG could be reduced by 82.8% and 73.5% respectively when the 1.0% CDs-PSS-M2070 was added. Additionally, CDs-PSS-M2070 as lubricant additive could work efficiently in a broad load. Impressively, carbon-dots based ionic fluid exhibited superior dispersion stability before and after frictional tests. The excellent dispersion and charged nature of the CDs-PSS-M2070 solvent-free ionic fluid in PEG helped continue to absorb on the frictional interface and generate continuous carbon-rich oil-films, which not only acted as a rolling effect but also showed a self-mending effect, thereby efficiently avoiding direct contact of the friction pairs and enhancing the friction-reducing and wear-resistance abilities.

Tao Cai conceived the idea, designed the experiment and wrote the manuscript. Dan Liu helped to analyze the data. Shenggao Liu supervised the research. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52075527 and 21404114), and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo (Grant No. 2019A610028). The authors are very appreciative of the tremendous revision in English by Edwin E. Liu, an ambitious high school sophomore at the Dougherty Valley High School, San Ramon, California, USA, with interdisciplinary interests in materials science and technology, finance, venture capital investment, stock trading, data science research, and public policy.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2021.108317.
